lines.greek: ἡρώων, τοῖσίν τε κοτέσσεται ὀβριμοπάτρη.
βῆ δὲ κατʼ Οὐλύμποιο καρήνων ἀίξασα,
στῆ δʼ Ἰθάκης ἐνὶ δήμῳ ἐπὶ προθύροις Ὀδυσῆος,
οὐδοῦ ἐπʼ αὐλείου· παλάμῃ δʼ ἔχε χάλκεον ἔγχος,
εἰδομένη ξείνῳ, Ταφίων ἡγήτορι Μέντῃ.
εὗρε δʼ ἄρα μνηστῆρας ἀγήνορας. οἱ μὲν ἔπειτα
πεσσοῖσι προπάροιθε θυράων θυμὸν ἔτερπον
ἥμενοι ἐν ῥινοῖσι βοῶν, οὓς ἔκτανον αὐτοί·
κήρυκες δʼ αὐτοῖσι καὶ ὀτρηροὶ θεράποντες
οἱ μὲν οἶνον ἔμισγον ἐνὶ κρητῆρσι καὶ ὕδωρ,
οἱ δʼ αὖτε σπόγγοισι πολυτρήτοισι τραπέζας
νίζον καὶ πρότιθεν, τοὶ δὲ κρέα πολλὰ δατεῦντο.
τὴν δὲ πολὺ πρῶτος ἴδε Τηλέμαχος θεοειδής,
ἧστο γὰρ ἐν μνηστῆρσι φίλον τετιημένος ἦτορ,
ὀσσόμενος πατέρʼ ἐσθλὸν ἐνὶ φρεσίν, εἴ ποθεν ἐλθὼν
μνηστήρων τῶν μὲν σκέδασιν κατὰ δώματα θείη,
τιμὴν δʼ αὐτὸς ἔχοι καὶ δώμασιν οἷσιν ἀνάσσοι.
τὰ φρονέων, μνηστῆρσι μεθήμενος, εἴσιδʼ Ἀθήνην.
βῆ δʼ ἰθὺς προθύροιο, νεμεσσήθη δʼ ἐνὶ θυμῷ
ξεῖνον δηθὰ θύρῃσιν ἐφεστάμεν· ἐγγύθι δὲ στὰς

her by-far first saw Telemachus, godlike-of-form,
for-he-was-sitting among the-wooers, his-dear heart grieved,
seeing his-noble father in his-mind, if from-somewhere coming
of-the-wooers a-scattering throughout the-halls he-might-make,

literal: of warriors, against whom she, daughter of a mighty sire, is angered.
Then she went darting down from the peaks of Olympus,
and stood in the land of Ithaca at the forecourt of Odysseus,
on the threshold of the court, holding the bronze spear in her palm,
in the likeness of a stranger, Mentes, leader of the Taphians.
There she found the proud wooers. At that moment they were
delighting their hearts with draughts before the doors,
sitting on the hides of oxen that they had slain themselves;
and their heralds and busy attendants were about them,
some mixing wine and water for them in the bowls,
while others washed the tables with porous sponges
and set them out, and others portioned meats in abundance.
Telemachus, godlike of form, was by far the first to see her,
for he sat among the wooers with a grieving heart,
seeing his noble father in his mind, if he might come from somewhere
and make a scattering of the wooers throughout the halls,
and himself hold honour and rule over his own house.
Pondering this as he sat among the wooers, he caught sight of Athena,
and went straight to the forecourt, indignant at heart
that a stranger should stand long at the doors. Standing near,
translations.elizabethan:
Down from Olympus' peaks she came in haste,
and stood in Ithaca at Odysseus' gate,
upon the courtyard sill, and in her hand
a spear of bronze; and she had made her seem
a stranger, Mentes, lord of Taphian men.
There found she those proud suitors, who at draughts
before the doors made merry, seated all
on hides of oxen they themselves had slain;
and heralds and quick servants moved among them,
some mingling wine with water in the bowls,
some wiping down the boards with porous sponge
and setting them, while others carved the meat.
Telemachus, in form a god, was first
by far to mark her, sitting there heart-sore
among the suitors, dreaming of his sire:
how, come from somewhere, he might scatter them
through all the halls, and take his honour back,
and rule his own house once again. So thinking,
he saw Athena, and he crossed at once
straight to the gate, ashamed within his heart
that any guest should wait so long without.
translations.modernist:
She came down from the peaks of Olympus and stood in Ithaca at the gate of Odysseus, on the threshold of the court. She held a bronze spear. She had made herself look like a stranger, Mentes, who led the Taphians.

The proud suitors were there. They were playing draughts in front of the doors, sitting on the hides of oxen they had killed themselves. Heralds and servants worked around them. Some mixed wine and water in the bowls. Some wiped the tables with sponges and set them out. Others carved the meat.

Telemachus saw her first. He was sitting with the suitors and his heart was heavy. He was thinking about his father. If the man came back from wherever he was, he would scatter these men through the house. He would have his honor again. He would rule what was his. He was thinking this when he saw Athena. He got up and went straight to the gate. It shamed him that a stranger had been left standing at the door.
translations.storybook:
Down she flew from the very top of Mount Olympus, all the way to Ithaca, and there she stood at the gate of Odysseus' house with a bronze spear in her hand. But she had made herself look like somebody else: a visitor named Mentes, who was chief of the Taphian people.

And what did she find there? The greedy suitors, playing a board game in front of the doors, sitting on the hides of the oxen they had killed and eaten themselves. Servants bustled all around them. Some mixed wine and water in the big bowls, some wiped the tables clean with sponges, and others carved up great heaps of meat.

Now, young Telemachus was the very first to see her. He was sitting among the suitors feeling terribly sad, thinking about his father, wishing and wishing that he might come home and scatter all these rude men out of the house, and take back the honour and the home that were his. That was what he was thinking when he caught sight of the stranger. He jumped up at once and hurried to the gate, for he was ashamed that a guest had been left waiting at the door.

murray.text: and she was in the likeness of a stranger, Mentes, the leader of the Taphians. There she found the proud wooers. They were taking their pleasure at draughts in front of the doors, sitting on the hides of oxen which they themselves had slain; and of the heralds and busy squires,

some were mixing wine and water for them in bowls, others again were washing the tables with porous sponges and setting them forth, while still others were portioning out meats in abundance. Her the godlike Telemachus was far the first to see, for he was sitting among the wooers, sad at heart,

seeing in thought his noble father, should he perchance come from somewhere and make a scattering of the wooers in the palace, and himself win honor and rule over his own house. As he thought of these things, sitting among the wooers, he beheld Athena, and he went straight to the outer door; for in his heart he counted it shame

that a stranger should stand long at the gates. So, drawing near, he clasped her right hand, and took from her the spear of bronze; and he spoke, and addressed her with winged words: “Hail, stranger; in our house thou shalt find entertainment and then, when thou hast tasted food, thou shalt tell of what thou hast need.”
